coredata.dc:title: The synergistic antioxidant effect of polydopamine coating with amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots on osteoblast protection against oxidative stress
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jieping
Li, Kai
Shao, Dandan
Ding, Yi
Huang, Liping
Zheng, Xuebin
coredata.dc:description: Oxidative stress is a major pathological feature in patients with metabolic bone diseases and isn’t conducive to postoperative osseointegration of implants. Although the free-radical scavenging activities of polydopamine (PDA) have been reported, its antioxidant-active catechol structure is susceptible to oxidation. Here, amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were introduced into PDA to protect catechol structure from oxidation and expand its free-radical scavenging capacity. Compared to the clinically used hydroxyapatite (HAp) coating and the PDA-HAp coating, the GQD/PDA-HAp coating exhibited higher capacity to scavenge ABTS·+, DPPH and O2 ·-. The enhanced antioxidant effect of the GQD/PDA-HAp coating was ascribed to the combined effects of the sp2-hybridized carbon in GQDs and more catechol groups in PDA, indicating the synergistic antioxidant relationship between GQDs and PDA. In addition, the amino-functionalized GQDs increased the catechol content and the reducibility of the GQD/PDA coating as confirmed by Folin-Ciocalteu colorimetric assay and mediated electrochemical probing. In vitro studies showed that the GQD/PDA-HAp coating minimized intracellular reactive oxygen species production and protected osteoblast viability against H2O2-induced oxidative stress. Taken together, introducing amino groups in PDA is an efficient approach to preserve its structural related antioxidant properties which may broaden its application in treating oxidative stress-related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polydopamine
Catechol
Graphene quantum dots
Antioxidant
Oxidative stress
Osteoblastic cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of amino-functionalized GQDs and GQD/PDA-HAp coating
Cell morphology observation and proliferation assay
Synthesis of amino-functionalized GQDs and surface characterization of GQD/PDA-HAp coating
Enhanced catalytic activity of GQD/PDA-HAp coating for free radical scavenging
The effect of amino-functionalized GQDs on the catechol structure and the redox-activity of PDA
Cytoprotective effects of the GQD/PDA-HAp coating against ROS-induced oxidative stress

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 51971236], the Shanghai Rising-Star Program [Grant No. 21QA1410400], the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality [Grant No. 21S31901200], and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of the Chinese Academy of Sciences [Grant No. 2020254].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the amine-functionalized GQDs. (A) Illustration of the preparation process. (B) TEM image of the GQDs (inset: inverse FFT of the high-resolution image). (C) UV–Visible spectrum of the GQDs (inset: the GQDs under UV and visible light illumination). (D) C 1s and (E) N 1s fitting curves of XPS spectra of the GQDs.
Surface characterization of the HAp, PDA-HAp and GQD/PDA-HAp coatings. (A) SEM images, (B) XRD patterns, (C) Raman spectra and (D) FTIR spectra.

Scavenging activities of the coatings against ABTS·+ and DPPH radicals. Absorbance of the solution after the 150-minute reaction of the coatings with (A) ABTS·+ and (C) DPPH radicals. Scavenging efficiency of the coatings on (B) ABTS·+ and (D) DPPH radicals at different time points.
Fitting curves XPS (A) O 1s spectra and (B) C 1s spectra of the as-prepared and DPPH-treated coatings.
(A) Folin-Ciocalteu assay of catechol content in the PDA-HAp and GQD/PDA-HAp coatings. (B) Fitting curves of O 1s spectra of the coatings after 14 days of oxidation in air.

(A) Schematic diagram of the redox cycling of PDA, GQD and GQD/PDA with mediators after application of cyclic voltage. (B) Cyclic voltammetric curves and (C) input voltage as well as amplified stable output current during the redox cycle of reduction and oxidation.

(A) Cell proliferation of H2O2-treated MC3T3-E1 osteoblasts on the coatings for 1, 4, and 7 days. (B) Adhesion morphology of H2O2-treated cells on the coatings for 1 day. The cells on HAp coating without H2O2 treatment served as control. (C) Immunofluorescence images of intracellular ROS on day 1 of the coatings by DCFH-DA assay. Scale bar = 50 μm. (D) Levels of intracellular ROS produced by cells cultured on the sample surfaces on days 7 and 14.

(A) Preparation of the plasma-sprayed HAp coating and GQD/PDA-HAp coating. (B) Synergistic antioxidant mechanism of the GQD/PDA-HAp coating against free radicals. (C) Cytoprotective effects of the coatings in terms of cell proliferation, cell adhesion and intracellular ROS under H2O2-induced oxidative stress.
The element content of the coatings before and after oxidation in air.

head.ce:title: The synergistic antioxidant effect of polydopamine coating with amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots on osteoblast protection against oxidative stress

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Inorganic Coating Materials CAS, Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 1295 Dingxi Road, Shanghai, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Inorganic Coating Materials CAS

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, 19 Yuquan Road, Beijing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, 19 Yuquan Road, Beijing, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Inorganic Coating Materials CAS, Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 1295 Dingxi Road, Shanghai, China (K. Li).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Inorganic Coating Materials CAS

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxidative stress is a major pathological feature in patients with metabolic bone diseases and isn’t conducive to postoperative osseointegration of implants. Although the free-radical scavenging activities of polydopamine (PDA) have been reported, its antioxidant-active catechol structure is susceptible to oxidation. Here, amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were introduced into PDA to protect catechol structure from oxidation and expand its free-radical scavenging capacity. Compared to the clinically used hydroxyapatite (HAp) coating and the PDA-HAp coating, the GQD/PDA-HAp coating exhibited higher capacity to scavenge ABTS·+, DPPH and O2 ·-. The enhanced antioxidant effect of the GQD/PDA-HAp coating was ascribed to the combined effects of the sp2-hybridized carbon in GQDs and more catechol groups in PDA, indicating the synergistic antioxidant relationship between GQDs and PDA. In addition, the amino-functionalized GQDs increased the catechol content and the reducibility of the GQD/PDA coating as confirmed by Folin-Ciocalteu colorimetric assay and mediated electrochemical probing. In vitro studies showed that the GQD/PDA-HAp coating minimized intracellular reactive oxygen species production and protected osteoblast viability against H2O2-induced oxidative stress. Taken together, introducing amino groups in PDA is an efficient approach to preserve its structural related antioxidant properties which may broaden its application in treating oxidative stress-related diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Plasma-sprayed hydroxyapatite (HAp) coating has been extensively used as surface coating on orthopedic and dental implants, due to its excellent biocompatibility and osteoconductivity[1,2]. However, HAp-coated Ti-based implants still have undesirable failure rates in the patients who are compromised by advanced age or chronic-degenerative disease[3,4]. Aging and more of 100 chronic-degenerative diseases have been linked to abnormal oxidative stress including metabolic bone disease among which osteoporosis is found. Inappropriate increase of reactive oxygen species (ROS) caused by oxidative stress inhibits osteoblast activity in the local fracture environment and thus adversely affects bone regeneration[5,6]. Therefore, imparting the HAp-coated Ti implants with antioxidant capability to scavenge excess ROS can improve post-operative outcomes in oxidative stress-mediated diseases[7,8].

Beyond the natural enzymes or inorganic nanozymes[9,10], in the last decades, increasing efforts have been devoted to explore novel and high-efficient antioxidant organic molecules[11], which possess high biocompatibility and ROS-scavenging properties. Among them, melanin-like polydopamine (PDA)[12,13], derived from the self-polymerization of dopamine, is the first adhesive material that can functionalize virtually any material surface, due to its material-independent surface modification property[14]. It is known that PDA nanomaterial and biopolymer can be employed for the treatment of oxidative stress-induced periodontal and bone diseases[15-17], which could be attributed to its effective free-radical scavenging by surface catechol residues[17]. Nevertheless, dopamine is highly susceptible to oxidation, and the number of catechol groups decreases gradually during the self-polymerization of dopamine monomer[18]. Recently, a couple of studies have illustrated that the reactivity of catechol structures on PDA surface can be tailored and improved by reducing agent treatment and amino acid molecular doping[19]. Previous evidence suggested that reducing treatment of ascorbic acid enhanced the scavenging efficiency of PDA toward ABTS·+ and DPPH radical when compared to the untreated one[20]. It has been reported that doped arginine could disrupt the planar stacked oligomeric structure within PDA and increase the accessibility of carboxyl groups to free radicals[21,22]. Moreover, amine groups in arginine could react readily with intermediates during the dopamine polymerization via Michael addition reaction and/or Schiff’s base reaction[23,24], which protect catechol groups from oxidation. Since PDA exhibits the chemical reactivity with nucleophiles such as amines, it makes doping amine-containing compounds into PDA feasible.

Although the free-radical scavenging activities of PDA have been reported, single-component antioxidant often fails to sufficiently prevent oxidative damage due to the presence of multiple ROS generated in the diseases progress including radical and non-radical oxygen species[25]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional nanomaterials with sp2 hybridized carbon atom layers and inherent targeting ability for functional groups (e.g. –OH, –NH2, etc.) that exhibit broad-spectrum ROS scavenging activities[26,27]. Surface modification of GQDs by functional groups is an efficient way to improve their performance. Amino-functionalized GQDs had been found to significantly broaden their light adsorption range[28]. Besides, the amino-functionalized GQDs can reduce subunits of PDA such as dihydroxyindole (DHI) and indole-5,6-quinone (IQ) through Michael addition and/or Schiff's base reaction, which protect catechol groups in PDA from oxidation. Therefore, doping the amino-functionalized GQDs in the cross-linked structure of PDA might have a synergistic antioxidant effect with PDA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of amino-functionalized GQDs and GQD/PDA-HAp coating
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of amino-functionalized GQDs was based on previous experiments[29]. Briefly, pyrene (1 g) was nitrated into 1,3,6-trinitropyrene in HNO3 (80 mL) solution at 80 °C under refluxing and stirring for 12 h. The yellow resultant (1.5 g) was dispersed in 0.4 M ammonia and 1.5 M hydrazine hydrate solution of DI water followed by ultrasonication. The suspension was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. The product containing water-soluble GQDs was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter, and then dialysed in a dialysis bag (3,500 Da) for 2 days.

HAp coatings were prepared on Ti-6Al-4 V plates using a vacuum plasma spraying system (F4-VB, Sulzer Metco, Switzerland)[30]. PDA and GQD/PDA were deposited on HAp coatings via a one-pot reaction of solution polymerization at room temperature for 24 h. The HAp coatings were added to a petri dish containing hydrochloric acid-dopamine (DA, 2 mg/mL) or DA (2 mg/mL) mixed with GQDs (1 mg/mL). And the pH was adjusted to approximately 8.5 by adding Tris buffers. After that, the petri dish was placed on a horizontal rotary shaker. The coating samples were ultrasonically cleaned with deionized water and dried in an oven. Finally, all the coating samples were stored in sealed bags in darkness.

Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Magellan 400, USA) was performed to observe the surface morphology of the coating samples. The phase composition and crystallinity of the samples were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/Max 2550 V, Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation. Raman spectra excited at a 785 nm laser line were collected by Raman spectrometer (Lab RAM HR Evolution, HORIBA Scientific, France). The atomic percentages of C, N and O content of freshly prepared as well as PDA -HAp and GQD/PDA-HAp coatings placed in air for 14 days were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) using a monochromatic Al Kα source to verify the degree of oxidation of the coatings. Surface charging was corrected considering adventitious C 1s (binding energies (BE) = 285 eV). Processing of the spectra was accomplished by XPSPEAK41 software. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum of GQDs were collected in a range of 200–600 nm by a spectrophotometer (UV-8000, Shanghai Metash Instruments Co., Ltd).

The amount of catechol in the coating was determined by Folin-Ciocalteu colorimetric method. Add 3 mL of deionized water and 0.125 mL of freshly prepared Folin reagent to a 4 mL round-bottom centrifuge tube. Several coatings were added or not as the experimental group and blank control group, respectively. After 2 h of reaction against light, the coating was removed and then 0.875 mL of 200 g/L solution of Na2CO3 was added to all centrifuge tubes. The golden yellow reagent changed to blue in the presence of the reducing agent catechol. The absorbance of the solution was measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometer at ∼760 nm.

7 mM ABTS solution and 3 mM K2S2O8 solution was mixed for 12 h at 4 °C in the dark to obtain a blue-green solution containing ABTS·+ radicals. And then diluted to 0.35 mM. The absorbance values of the samples at ∼734 nm were measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer. A purple solution of 0.1 mM DPPH was prepared using anhydrous ethanol. Test the absorbance values of the samples at ∼517 nm by UV–vis spectrophotometer. The ABTS·+ and DPPH scavenging activities of the samples were obtained every 30 min by using the following formula:
Scavenging activity (%) = (Abs control - Abs sample )/Abs control × 100 %

We used the inhibition and produce superoxide anion assay kit (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute) to determine the O2 ·- scavenging ability of the samples. The kit simulates the reaction system of xanthine and xanthine oxygenase in the organism, which generates superoxide anion radicals. The absorbance at 550 nm was measured using a spectrophotometer after 30 min of reaction in the dark.

The effect of GQDs on the electron gain and loss capacity of PDA in redox media was evaluated using a three-electrode electrochemical workstation (Corr Test, CS) with linear cyclic voltammetry[31]. The working electrode was gold sheet (10 × 10 × 0.1 mm), the counter electrode was platinum sheet (10 × 10 × 0.1 mm), and the saturated glycerol electrodes was used as the reference electrode.

A thin chitosan hydrogel film was electrodeposited onto a gold electrode in the fully swollen chitosan solution after electrodepositing at −4 mA/cm2 for 120 s. To prepare PDA, GQD and GQD/PDA grafted films, chitosan-coated electrode was immersed in 0.1 M phosphate buffer containing dopamine (2 mg/mL), GQDs (1 mg/mL) or both of them, separately. And then an anodic potential was applied to the underlying electrode (0.6 V, 5 min). This grafted film can simulate the natural oxidative polymerization process of PDA in air.

The 50 μM of two electrochemical mediators, Ru(NH3)6Cl3 and 1,1′-Ferrocenedimethanol (Fc) were prepared with 0.1 M PBS. And the film-coated electrode to be tested was immersed in it. The imposed voltage was cycled between −0.4 V and +0.5 V vs saturated glycerol electrode (SCE).

Mouse osteoblastic cell line MC3T3-E1 was obtained from the Cell Bank of Type Culture Collection of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). The cells were maintained in α-MEM (Hy Clone, USA) supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin (complete α-MEM) at 37 °C under humidified condition of 5 % CO2. Oxidative stress was induced by treating MC3T3-E1 cells with 0.3 mM H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell morphology observation and proliferation assay
ce:section.ce:para: The sterile samples were placed in 48-well cell culture plates. 1 mL of cell suspension (5 × 103 cells/mL) was inoculated on the surface of each well material as the normal group. The oxidative stress group was added with H2O2 containing 0.3 mM. After 24 h of incubation, the samples were washed with PBS and soaked in glutaraldehyde (2 %, Sigma) to fix cells. After complete dehydration of the samples at 37 °C with a series of concentration gradients of ethanol (30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 %, 100 %), the morphology of the cells was observed under FE-SEM.

The CCK-8 kit (Tongren institute of chemistry, Japan) was used to measure the proliferation of MC3T3-E1 cells on the coating surface. Briefly, Cells were inoculated on the coatings in 48-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well with or without H2O2 treatment. After 1, 4 and 7 days of incubation, the culture solution was discarded and a mixture of 1 mL of fresh culture solution and 0.1 mL of CCK-8 solution was added. The OD value of each well was measured at 450 nm by an enzyme marker. Three hours later, the absorbance at 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader (Bio-Tek, USA).

The effect of specimens on intracellular ROS in MC3T3-E1 cells was tested by ROS immunofluorescence photographs and intracellular ROS content. Cells were inoculated on the coatings in 48-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well with or without H2O2 treatment. After 24 h, the supernatant was discarded and the cells were washed twice with PBS. A final concentration of 10 µM DCFH-DA solution was added to each well and incubated for 20 min at 37 °C. The supernatant was then discarded and the cells were washed 3 times with PBS. Cells were fixed with 2 % paraformaldehyde for 30 min and then observed by laser confocal microscopy. Measurement of intracellular ROS content was performed in a similar manner as described above. After 7 and 14 days of incubation, cells were stained with 25 μM DCFH-DA for 45 min at room temperature. After washing 3 times, 0.3 mL of cell lysis solution was added to each well and the cells were lysed at 37 °C for 10 min. The mixed lysis solution was then added to a 96-well plate and the fluorescence intensity value at 525 nm was read by the fluorescent enzyme marker.

The data were presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD) of three parallel experiments (n = 3). Statistical differences were analyzed by GraphPad Prism 8.0 using two-way ANOVA with Sidak’s multiple comparisons test. Significance was indicated by *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, or ***P < 0.001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amino-functionalized GQDs and surface characterization of GQD/PDA-HAp coating
ce:section.ce:para: The amino-functionalized GQDs were prepared through a bottom-up approach (Fig. 1 A). The Raman spectrum of the synthesized reactant 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was shown in Fig. S1, supporting information. TEM image (Fig. 1B) revealed the presence of nanodots with an average diameter of about 3.02 nm. Lattice fringe distance of 0.257 nm corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon was observed by HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1B). As shown in Fig. 1C, the UV–vis spectrum of the synthesized GQDs exhibited a clear peak at about 237 nm and a broad visible absorption band. The UV absorption peaks were related to π-π*/n-π* electronic transitions of C=C, C=N, and C=O bonds in the graphene structure, while the visible absorption peaks could be related to the partial conjugated π electrons in the layered structure of the GQDs[32]. The inset photograph in Fig. 1C showed that the amino-functionalized GQDs solution was yellowish under daylight and emitted a bright blue luminescence under UV illumination. In the Raman spectrum of the GQDs (Fig. S2, supporting information), there was a typical G band at 1580 cm−1 corresponding to graphitic carbon (sp2-C) and a disorder D band at 1350 cm−1 ascribed to sp3 defects. The value of the ID/IG ratio of 0.81 indicated crystallographic structure[33]. The structure and surface state of the amino-functionalized GQDs was further characterized by XPS. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. S3, supporting information) showed strong signals of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s. The atom ratio of N and O was determined as 15.20 at. % and 16.85 at. %, respectively. The XPS spectra fitting results of GQDs were listed in Table S1, supporting information. As shown in Fig. 1D, the deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s showed three main peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.1 eV, and 287.9 eV, corresponding to C–C and graphitic C=C, C-O and C-N functions, and C=O groups, respectively[34]. The deconvoluted N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1E) consisted of two peaks at 401.9 eV and 399.6 eV, which were assigned to aromatic N and surface H2N groups, respectively. Much higher percent of surface H2N groups indicated the covalent anchorage of amino groups at the surface of GQDs[29].

Fig. 2 A showed the optic macrophages and surface morphologies of the HAp, PDA-HAp and GQD/PDA-HAp coatings. The HAp coating surface showed typical plasma-sprayed morphology composed of molten droplets and semi-molten structure. Surface modification of HAp coating by PDA and GQD/PDA film turned the white color of the coating into brown and gray, respectively. At higher magnification SEM images, a layer consisted of discrete nanodots was observed after the deposition of PDA and GQD/PDA on the HAp coating. The average thickness of the PDA and GQD/PDA layers was 210.67 and 15.00 nm, respectively, which was determined by cross-section images of the coatings (Fig. S4, supporting information). The significant difference in PDA coating thickness indicated that the addition of GQD inhibited dopamine polymerization probably through preventing catechol groups from oxidation. The XRD pattern of the HAp coating in Fig. 2B showed that the diffraction peaks were well-indexed to hydroxyapatite (PDF#74–0565). After the deposition of PDA and GQD/PDA on the HAp coating, the intensity of the diffraction peaks of HAp became weaker. This was probably due to the masking of the HAp signal by PDA layer. As shown in Fig. 2C, the characteristic G and D bands for carbon appeared in the Raman spectra of the PDA-HAp and GQD/PDA-HAp coatings. The ID/IG ratio of the GQD/PDA-HAp coating (0.89) was slightly lower than that of the PDA-HAp coating (0.92). The successful deposition of the PDA and GQD/PDA layers was also confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2D). FTIR spectra of the PDA-HAp and GQD/PDA-HAp coatings both showed two peaks at 1616 cm−1 and 1503 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of C=C and C=N of aromatic amine in PDA[17,35], respectively. The roughness values of the HAp, PDA-HAp and GQD/PDA-HAp coatings (Fig. S5, supporting information) were 5.06 ± 0.08 μm, 5.56 ± 0.29 μm and 5.47 ± 0.30 μm, respectively. It indicated that PDA or PDA/GQD layers did not affect the surface roughness of HAp. The survey XPS spectra (Fig. S6, supporting information) revealed that the signals of Ca and P elements in the HAp coating, indicating that the presence of PDA and GQD/PDA layers could mask the signal of the underlying HAp coating. The signals of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s in the PDA-HAp and GQD/PDA-HAp coatings were reasonably ascribed to the PDA and GQD/PDA layers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced catalytic activity of GQD/PDA-HAp coating for free radical scavenging
ce:section.ce:para: Three typical free radicals, ABTS·+, DPPH and O2 ·-, were used to investigate the antioxidant properties of the as-prepared coatings. As shown in Fig. 3 A, compared with the blank control and the HAp coating, the PDA-HAp coating significantly reduced the absorbance of ABTS·+ solution at 734 nm demonstrating the ABTS·+ scavenging property of the PDA layer. The addition of the amino-functionalized GQDs further enhanced the ABTS·+ scavenging activity of the GQD/PDA-HAp coating. Fig. 3B revealed that the ABTS·+ clearance rate of the coating samples increased with the extension of incubation time. The GQD/PDA-HAp coating eliminate more than 70 % of ABTS·+ after 150 min, which was 1.97-fold and 1.21-fold of the HAp and PDA-HAp coatings, respectively. It was notable that the HAp coating exhibited certain removal efficiency. This was probably because the negatively charged HAp coating surface removed the positively charged ABTS·+ through an electron transfer mechanism. The results of DPPH radical scavenging (Fig. 3C and D) were consistent with those of ABTS·+ scavenging. Namely, the DPPH scavenging activities of the coatings increased in the order of HAp, PDA-HAp, and GQD/PDA-HAp. As a precursor of all reactive oxygen radicals, O2 ·- is a major free radical in living organisms. Fig. S7 (supporting information) displayed that the GQD/PDA-HAp showed the strongest O2 ·- scavenging ability among the three coatings.

Compounding PDA with other antioxidants is an effective way to improve its ROS-scavenging activities. For example, co-synthesizing PDA with selenocysteines (SeCys) exhibited better SOD- and catalase-mimetic activities and additional GPx enzymatic activity[36]. PDA-loaded ultrasmall Pt and Cu nanoparticles could act as nanozymes to scavenge ROS and provide excellent protection to chondrocytes under H2O2-stimulated oxidative stress[37].

The ABTS·+ and O2 ·- scavenging activities of the amino-functionalized GQDs were confirmed by using various nanoparticle concentration (Figs. S8 and 9, supporting information). This indicated that the ROS-scavenging activities of GQDs contributed to enhanced catalytic activity of GQD/PDA-HAp. To further distinguish the antioxidant effect of GQDs from that of PDA in GQD/PDA-HAp, we compared the C 1s and O 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of the as-prepared coatings and the DPPH-treated coatings[23,38]. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the O–H content in PDA-HAp coating decreased from 69.24 % to 49.29 % after treatment with DPPH radical. This suggested that the PDA-HAp coating removed the DPPH mainly through consumption of the catechol groups. In contrast, the GQD/PDA-HAp coating showed a significantly lower degree of the O–H content decrease (Fig. 4A). As shown in Fig. 4B, the content of C=C/C–C in the GQD/PDA-HAp coating was significantly decreased compared with that in the PDA-HAp coating. These results indicated that the GQD/PDA-HAp coating could scavenge the DPPH through consumption of the sp2-hybridized carbon in GQDs and the catechol groups in PDA. Previous studies have also demonstrated that the antioxidant properties of GQDs were mainly ascribed to the sp2-hybridized carbon. Li et al. [39] reported that phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dots exhibited excellent scavenging activity of free radicals due to the sp2-hybridized carbon. And this intrinsic structure of GQDs could scavenge ROS by forming adducts with ROS, undergoing electron transfer and hydrogen donation[40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of amino-functionalized GQDs on the catechol structure and the redox-activity of PDA
ce:section.ce:para: It is well-known that the catechol groups are redox-active sites in PDA[15,42]. We further characterized the redox-activity of the PDA and GQD/PDA layer using a mediated electrochemical probing (MEP) method (Fig. 6 A). Specifically, the MEP method[43,44] used controlled voltage inputs and two soluble mediators to shuttle electrons between the electrode and insoluble samples (embedded in chitosan film on the electrode). The output current was associated with mediator oxidation and reduction. The amplified mediator current was a signature pattern indicating redox-activity of the chitosan film-entrapped material. In our experiments, PDA, GQDs and GQD/PDA were embedded in chitosan film adjacent to a gold electrode, respectively. The chitosan-coated electrode was immersed in a solution containing two redox mediators: one oxidative mediator (500 μM ferrocene dimethanol (Fc), E 0 = + 0.25 V vs. Ag/AgCl) and one reductive mediator (500 μM Ru3+, E 0 = -0.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl). Cyclic voltage sequences (between −0.4 V and +0.5 V vs. SCE, 10 cycles, 50 mV/s) were imposed to the underlying electrode to initiate Fc oxidation or Ru3+ reduction.

Fig. 6B showed the cross-plot between output current and input voltage (i-V) with a single input voltage cycle. When the voltage was cycled to oxidative potentials (between 0 V and 0.5 V, mimicking oxidative physiological activities), Fc was oxidized and small oxidative output currents were observed for the electrodes coated by the chitosan and the GQD-embedded chitosan. This indicated that there was minimal (if any) direct electron transfer between GQDs and the oxidative mediator. The electrical output showed that chitosan films containing either PDA or GQD/PDA had amplified oxidative currents. The amplified currents were a signature of oxidative redox-cycling of Fc, which indicated that PDA and GQD/PDA could donate electrons to Fc+. This was agreement with previous studies that Fc was capable of undergoing oxidative redox-cycling reactions that shuttled electrons from the melanin to the electrode. The amplification of Fc oxidation for the film containing GQD/PDA was considerably stronger than that for the PDA-containing film. This indicated that the addition of amino-functionalized GQDs in PDA could enhance the reducibility of PDA through increasing the content of catechol groups. When the imposed voltage was cycled to be reductive, the electrochemical currents associated with Ru3+ reduction was observed to be amplified for the films containing PDA and GQD/PDA. There was no significant difference in the reductive currents between the PDA- and GQD/PDA-containing films. It suggested that the amino-functionalized GQDs exerted little effect on electron-accepting capacity of the GQD/PDA. As shown in Fig. 6C, the output response of the GQD/PDA-embedded film showed near steady amplified currents over the entire 6-min period of the experiment, which indicated that the GQD/PDA was reversibly redox-active and can be repeatedly oxidized and reduced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytoprotective effects of the GQD/PDA-HAp coating against ROS-induced oxidative stress
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 A depicted the proliferation of H2O2-treated MC3T3-E1 osteoblastic cells on the coating surfaces for 1, 7 and 14 days. It was observed that the addition of H2O2 inhibited the cell proliferation on the surface of HAp coating. The presence of PDA and GQD/PDA coatings significantly improved the proliferation of H2O2-treated cells at the predetermined time point. The H2O2-treated cells on the GQD/PDA-HAp coating showed the highest level of cell proliferation compared to those on the HAp and PDA-HAp coatings. Moreover, the proliferation level of H2O2-treated cells on the GQD/PDA-HAp coating was even higher than that of untreated cells on the HAp coating. The GQD/PDA-HAp coating had the best biocompatibility among all samples under normal culture conditions (Fig. S9, supporting information). The cytoprotective effect of the GQD/PDA-HAp coating on ROS-impaired cell adhesion was confirmed by SEM images (Fig. 7B). H2O2 treatment reduced the cell adhesion area and the pseudopod number of cells on the HAp coating. The presence of PDA and GQD/PDA layer preserved the cell morphology similar to that for the HAp coating without H2O2 treatment. The effect of ROS on cell adhesion morphology was also reported by Shen et al.[37]. Under the condition of high ROS, the cells are shuttle-shaped, with small spreading area and clear borders. When the excess ROS is removed, the cells have a large spreading area on the material surfaces and the borders are more blurred due to a large number of filamentous pseudopods.

To examine the effect of the coatings on the intracellular ROS content, we obtained the immunofluorescence images of the cells on the coatings for 1 day and the level of ROS production in cells on the coatings for 7 and 14 days. As shown in Figure 7C, H2O2-treated cells on the HAp surface showed significantly stronger fluorescence intensity than those without H2O2 treatment. Compared with the HAp + H2O2 group, the fluorescence intensity of the PDA-HAp + H2O2 and GQD/PDA-HAp + H2O2 groups diminished sequentially, which was consistent with the quantitative results of the intracellular ROS content (Fig. 7D). The GQD/PDA-HAp coating had the greatest effect on inhibiting the level of ROS production in H2O2-treated cells, almost restoring intracellular ROS to the level of normal culture conditions after 14 days of incubation. We have to be aware that in patients with metabolic bone disease and aging, the pathological condition of oxidative stress affects the long-term stability of the implant material and surrounding tissues, promoting bone resorption by activating multiple signaling pathways that mediate apoptosis of osteoblasts and osteocytes[45,46]. Researchers have found that the addition of antioxidants such as ascorbic acid, vitamins, and enzymes can reduce intracellular ROS levels and increase osteoblast activity, which helps accelerate osseointegration of implants[8,41,47]. Our experimental results also suggested that GQD/PDA-HAp coating could protect osteoblast activity from the negative effects of oxidative stress by scavenging intracellular ROS.

In this study, we deposited amino-functionalized GQDs-doped PDA layer on the surface of plasma-sprayed HAp coating. The GQD/PDA-HAp coating exhibited superior free-radical scavenging activity when compared with the PDA-HAp and HAp coatings. The enhanced antioxidant effect of the GQD/PDA-HAp coating was not only ascribed to the presence of the GQDs, but to the increased catechol structure in PDA protected by amino groups. MEP analysis demonstrated that the GQD/PDA coating with higher content of catechol groups exhibited higher reducibility, indicating that the GQD/PDA coating could easily donate electrons to oxidative free radicals. The GQD/PDA-HAp coating efficiently inhibited the level of ROS production in H2O2-treated cells, exhibiting the best ability to promote osteoblast proliferation and support osteoblast adhesion. The results indicated that the GQD/PDA-HAp coating held potential to ameliorate osseointegration of implants in oxidative stress-related diseases.

Jieping Li: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Kai Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Dandan Shao: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yi Ding: Resources, Software. Liping Huang: Methodology, Supervision. Xuebin Zheng: Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 51971236], the Shanghai Rising-Star Program [Grant No. 21QA1410400], the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality [Grant No. 21S31901200], and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of the Chinese Academy of Sciences [Grant No. 2020254].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.155950.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
